question:
Authoritative, practical knowledge base for GLUTEN-FREE/CELIAC and VEGAN travelers eating at the three major Japanese convenience-store (konbini) chains — 7-Eleven (Seven & i), FamilyMart, and Lawson. Goal: tell a traveler WHAT standard, year-round items they can safely eat, WHERE in the store to find them, and HOW to verify safety from the label. Use correct romaji + kanji and cite authoritative sources. Be CONSERVATIVE and precise — wrong "safe" calls are dangerous for celiacs; flag uncertainty explicitly. Cover:

1. JAPAN ALLERGEN LABELING LAW (the foundation): the 8 mandatory "specified allergens" (特定原材料) that MUST be declared on packaged foods, including wheat (小麦); how the 原材料名 / アレルギー (allergen) line works on konbini packaging; the difference between mandatory-declared (義務) and recommended-only (推奨, 20 items) allergens; what "本品製造工場では小麦を含む製品を製造" (made in a facility that also processes wheat) cross-contamination statements mean for celiacs. The exact Japanese label terms to scan for.

2. GLUTEN-FREE REALITY at konbini: precisely why most konbini staples are NOT safe (soy sauce 醤油 contains wheat; dashi; onigiri fillings; battered/fried hot-case items; natto tare; sashimi soy sachets; imitation crab; many sauces). Then the items that ARE plausibly gluten-free as standard stock, by category and chain where possible: plain salted/umeboshi onigiri (and which fillings to avoid), plain steamed rice, boiled eggs (味付け卵 caution), edamame, plain salads (dressing separate — check), cut fruit, plain yogurt, sashimi (with GF tamari, not the wheat-soy sachet), grilled/plain fish (check teriyaki glaze), 焼き芋 sweet potato, mochi/rice sweets and wagashi/anko (check), nuts, konnyaku, plain potato/corn, naturally-GF drinks. For each, the label term that confirms or disqualifies it (小麦 present?).

3. VEGAN REALITY at konbini: why katsuo/niboshi fish dashi and egg/dairy/gelatin are pervasive even in "vegetable" items. Then plausibly-vegan standard stock by category: certain onigiri (kombu, umeboshi, salt — but verify dashi), inarizushi (check), edamame, salads (no egg/meat/dairy + dressing), cut fruit, nuts, plain mochi & anko wagashi, umeboshi, roasted sweet potato, dark chocolate, certain potato chips/snacks, soy milk and plant drinks, plain rice. The animal-derived terms to scan for in 原材料名.

4. STORE LAYOUT / WHERE TO FIND IT: the typical konbini floor plan and where each safe category physically sits — onigiri & bento wall, chilled deli (salads, eggs, natto, tofu), the register hot-case (oden, fried chicken — mostly NOT safe), bread/pastry aisle (avoid for GF), snack/nuts aisle, frozen case, drinks wall, fresh fruit/produce (limited). So a traveler can go straight to the right spots.

5. CHAIN-SPECIFIC notes: 7-Eleven (Seven Premium private label), FamilyMart (Famima), Lawson (incl. Natural Lawson / Lawson's health-oriented & "Loffice" lines, and their published allergen/nutrition info on the official site or in-store kiosk). Which chain publishes the most usable allergen data online (allergen search by product), and how a traveler can look it up.

6. EXACT JAPANESE PHRASES the traveler can READ on packaging and SAY to staff (with romaji) for both GF ("contains wheat?" / "I can't eat wheat 小麦アレルギー") and vegan (no meat/fish/egg/dairy/fish-dashi). And whether konbini staff can reliably help (mostly not — emphasize self-service label reading).

Prioritize DURABLE, verifiable category-level guidance over ephemeral specific SKUs (which change seasonally), but name concrete reliable items where well-documented. Flag anything disputed or where online info is outdated/unreliable.
summary: Synthesis step was skipped or failed — returning 20 verified claims unmerged.

confirmed.claim: Food sold in Japan must be labelled in Japanese, which means allergen declarations (e.g., wheat 小麦) on konbini packaging appear only in Japanese and must be read in Japanese by travelers.
confirmed.quote: When selling in Japan, Food labelling must be in Japanese

confirmed.claim: As of this peer-reviewed paper (2021), Japan mandates allergen labeling for 7 specified ingredients (egg, cow's milk, wheat, buckwheat, peanut, shrimp, crab), with wheat mandatory since April 1, 2002. (Note: walnut was later added in 2023, raising the count to 8 — confirming the research question's '8 mandatory' figure but post-dating this source.)
confirmed.quote: 7 allergens require mandatory labeling: Egg, Cow's milk, Wheat, Buckwheat, Peanut, Shrimp, Crab ... Yes, wheat is mandatory as of April 1, 2002.

confirmed.claim: Beyond the mandatory list, Japan recommends (but does not require) labeling for an additional ~21 allergens, including soybean, sesame, gelatin, beef, pork, chicken, and several fruits/nuts — meaning these may be undeclared on konbini packaging.
confirmed.quote: 21 allergens are recommended for labeling, including: abalone, almond, squid, salmon roe, orange, cashew nut, kiwi fruit, beef, walnut, sesame, salmon, mackerel, soybean, chicken, banana, pork, matsutake mushroom, peach, yam, apple, and gelatin.

confirmed.claim: As of February 2025, Japan's Consumer Affairs Agency (CAA) mandates allergy labeling for eight 'Specific Ingredient' (特定原材料) items, and wheat is one of them.
confirmed.quote: As of February 2025, the CAA requires allergy labeling for eight “Specific Ingredient” items (Table 1).

confirmed.claim: The eight mandatory specified allergens are shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat (soba), egg, milk, and peanut.
confirmed.quote: Specific Ingredients (Mandatory Allergy Labeling) | shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat (soba), egg, milk, peanut | Frequent and/or severe cases

confirmed.claim: In addition to the mandatory list, the CAA highly recommends (but does not legally require) allergy labeling for 21 'Quasi-Specific Ingredient' (特定原材料に準ずるもの) items, including soybean, sesame, gelatin, beef, pork, chicken, salmon, mackerel, and squid.
confirmed.quote: In addition, the CAA highly recommends allergy labeling for 21 “Quasi-Specific Ingredient” items.

confirmed.claim: Japan mandates allergen labelling for exactly 8 specified ingredients (特定原材料) that must be declared on packaged foods: shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat, egg, milk, and peanut. Wheat (小麦) is one of these 8 mandatory allergens, so packaged konbini foods containing wheat must declare it — making the kanji 小麦 the single most important term for a celiac to scan for.
confirmed.quote: Specified ingredients / Labelling is mandatory: shrimp, crab, walnut, wheat, buckwheat, egg, milk, peanut. Labelling requirement: Labelling is mandatory if the packaged food includes these allergens.

confirmed.claim: A second tier of allergens has only RECOMMENDED (not mandatory) labelling, so these may be absent from a label even when present — relevant for vegans/celiacs because items like soybean, beef, pork, chicken, gelatin, mackerel and salmon fall in this voluntary tier and need not be declared.
confirmed.quote: Labelling is recommended: almond, abalone, squid, salmon roe, orange, cashew nut, kiwi fruit, beef, sesame, salmon, mackerel, soybean, chicken, banana, pork, macadamia nut, peach, wild yam, apple, gelatin ... The labelling may not show these items even if they are part of a packaged food.

confirmed.claim: The official CAA sheet supplies the kanji/kana for each of the 8 mandatory allergens that travelers can read on labels or point to with staff: えび (shrimp), かに (crab), くるみ (walnut), 小麦 (wheat), そば (buckwheat), 卵 (egg), 乳 (milk), 落花生/ピーナッツ (peanut), plus the phrase 私はアレルギーを持っています。この食品には下の表で印をつけたアレルゲンを含んでいますか? ('I have allergies. Does this food contain the allergens marked below?').
confirmed.quote: 8 specified ingredients (must be listed): えび / かに / くるみ / 小麦 / そば / 卵 / 乳 / 落花生(ピーナッツ). ❶ 私はアレルギーを持っています。この食品には下の表で印をつけたアレルゲンを含んでいますか?

confirmed.claim: Lawson's official allergen portal declares allergen status for the 8 legally-mandated specified allergens (特定原材料8品目) — which explicitly include wheat (小麦) — plus the 20 recommended-declaration items (準ずる20品目, which include soy 大豆, gelatin ゼラチン, beef, pork, chicken, etc.), for a total of 28 substances.
confirmed.quote: アレルギー情報の項目は、食品表示法にて表示が義務づけられている特定原材料８品目「えび、かに、くるみ、小麦、そば、卵、乳成分、落花生」と表示が推奨されている特定原材料に準ずる20品目「アーモンド、あわび、いか、いくら、オレンジ、カシューナッツ、キウイフルーツ、牛肉、ごま、さけ、さば、大豆、鶏肉、バナナ、豚肉、マカダミアナッツ、もも、やまいも、りんご、ゼラチン」の28品目です。

confirmed.claim: Lawson explicitly warns that products may be manufactured/cooked/sold using shared equipment with other allergen-containing products (i.e., cross-contamination), and that the published allergen data is only a reference for menu selection and does NOT guarantee that an allergic reaction will not occur — travelers must always confirm the on-pack label themselves and consult a physician.
confirmed.quote: 他のアレルギー物質を含む商品と共有の設備で製造・調理・販売しているものもございます。…記載のアレルゲンの有無の表示は、お客さまのメニュー選択のご参考にしていただくものであり、アレルギー症状が発症しないことを保証するものではございません。ご購入に際しては、専門医にご相談の上、お客さまご自身でご判断いただきますようお願いいたします。

confirmed.claim: FamilyMart's official allergen hub lists the 8 mandatory specified allergens (特定原材料) as shrimp, crab, wheat (小麦), buckwheat, egg, dairy, peanut, and walnut — confirming wheat is a legally mandatory-declared allergen on packaging.
confirmed.quote: 特定原材料8品目（えび、かに、小麦、そば、卵、乳成分、落花生（ピーナッツ）、くるみ）

confirmed.claim: FamilyMart explicitly states its published allergen listings are only a reference for menu selection and do NOT guarantee an allergic reaction will not occur — meaning travelers cannot treat the online data as a safety guarantee.
confirmed.quote: 記載のアレルゲンの有無の表示は、お客さまのメニュー選択のご参考にしていただくものであり、アレルギー症状が発症しないことを保証するものではございません。

confirmed.claim: FamilyMart directs customers to verify the product label and in-store POP signage at the point of purchase, reinforcing that self-service label reading is the authoritative verification method.
confirmed.quote: 購入の際には商品ラベル・店頭POPをご確認いただきますようお願いいたします

confirmed.claim: 7-Eleven Japan publishes specified-allergen (特定原材料), nutritional, and calorie information for its products via this official allergen/nutrition hub page.
confirmed.quote: 特定原材料や栄養成分、カロリーを表示しています

confirmed.claim: Plain salted onigiri (rice balls) are gluten-free safe, while tuna mayo onigiri fillings often contain soy sauce and are therefore not safe.
confirmed.quote: Salt/Plain: Just plain salted rice. 100% safe ... Tuna Mayo fillings often contain soy sauce

confirmed.claim: Konbini soy sauce (醤油 / shoyu) contains wheat, which is why staple items like oden broth and bento sauces are not gluten-free.
confirmed.quote: Oden stew: broth contains conventional soy sauce (wheat); 小麦 (Komugi) = Wheat — marked DANGER; 醤油 (Shoyu) = Soy Sauce — marked DANGER

confirmed.claim: At Japanese konbini, onigiri (rice balls) vary by filling — plum (umeboshi) and sweet-bean/sesame fillings are noted as wheat-free, while some salted rice balls contain vinegar (a possible gluten source) and certain salmon onigiri (e.g., at MiniStop) contain wheat; each must be label-checked individually.
confirmed.quote: similar items contain wheat and others don't ... plum rice balls or the ones with sweet beans and sesame seeds ... One ... salted rice ball ... included vinegar, which can sometimes contain gluten ... MiniStop's salmon rice ball contains wheat

confirmed.claim: Salads/steamed-bean packs may be safe plain but the dressings are almost always not GF, some 'healthy' salads add unlabeled barley, and potato salad contains vinegar making it questionable.
confirmed.quote: the dressings are almost always not ... some 'healthy' salads will add barley, which won't be clearly marked ... does not contain wheat, but it does contain vinegar, which makes it questionable

confirmed.claim: 7-Eleven does not stock certified or clearly-labeled gluten-free staples (GF bread, GF soy sauce, GF snacks/desserts/dinners/pastas), and essentially nothing the author recommends is certified gluten-free — verification relies on self-service label reading via translation.
confirmed.quote: GF bread, GF soy sauce, clearly-labeled GF snacks, desserts, dinners, pastas, etc. ... almost nothing I'm going to talk about here is certified gluten-free

refuted.claim: Hidden wheat/gluten is pervasive in konbini snacks because soy sauce is added even to chip products, and many sweets/ice creams contain mizuame; e.g. Calbee salted potato sticks are safe only in the green package, and 'even Japanese Doritos have soy sauce in them.'

Japanese soy sauce (醤油 shoyu) standardly contains wheat flour, making it a primary hidden gluten source that renders otherwise gluten-free konbini dishes unsafe for celiacs; even tamari contains wheat in Japan.
Plain pickled-plum (ume) and some salmon (鮭 sake) onigiri were found gluten-free, but most konbini onigiri are off-limits due to added soy sauce.
Celiac travelers should scan konbini ingredient lists for specific kanji including wheat 小麦/小麦粉 (komugi/komugiko), barley 麦/大麦, soy sauce 醤油, malt 麦芽, and wheat gluten 麩 (fu).
Plain salt onigiri at all three major chains (7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson) are vegan, but all other flavored onigiri may contain hidden fish that is not declared on the ingredient list.
